title: Drawknife shaping technique
subtitle: Shaving green-wood stock to profile on the shaving horse
excerpt: The drawknife is a two-handled blade pulled toward the body to remove material rapidly from green-wood billets. Used on a shaving horse, it can produce a chair leg, an axe handle, or a roof shingle from rough riven stock in a fraction of the time a knife alone would take. The technique is simple in principle but requires reading grain direction continuously and controlling depth of cut with body leverage rather than hand pressure.
sourceNotes: Drawknife and shaving-horse technique documented in Cassell's Cyclopaedia of Mechanics vol. III (1885) and in British chairmaking and coopering craft traditions.

glossaryTerms.definition: A two-handled blade with a bevel on one face, pulled toward the body to shave material from green-wood stock. Removes wood faster than a sloyd knife and slower than an axe; used to refine riven billets to near-final profile.

A foot-operated bench with a pivoting clamp. The maker sits astride the bench, rests the work against the clamp head, and presses a foot pedal to lock the clamp; releasing foot pressure frees the work to be repositioned.

Freshly felled wood still high in moisture content. Green wood shaves more easily than seasoned under the drawknife and is the correct material for most drawknife work; seasoned wood requires considerably more effort.
The angled face ground on one side of a blade. The drawknife's bevel can face upward or downward in use; bevel down produces a shallower, more controlled cut; bevel up produces a more aggressive cut.
Split along the grain. Riven stock is the correct starting material for drawknife work; the straight grain allows long, clean shavings without running into opposing fibres.

content.text: are the primary production tools of the green-woodworker. A

ash that would take an hour with a sloyd knife can be brought to near-final profile in fifteen minutes on a shaving horse. The technique is straightforward: sit, clamp, pull. The skill lies in reading grain direction and adjusting the angle of the blade's
to control the depth of cut.

Set the shaving-horse clamp height to match the diameter of the stock being worked. The work should sit comfortably between the clamp head and the working platform with the clamp gripping firmly under light foot pressure. The maker sits astride the bench with feet on the treadle, knees roughly level with the working surface. Test the clamp with a sharp forward pull on the work before starting: the clamp should tighten when pulled, not release.

Both hands grip the drawknife handles with an overhand grip. The blade is held parallel to the work surface at the beginning of each cut. The pull comes from the upper body and arms together, not just the hands. Lean back slightly from the waist at the beginning of a long shaving stroke and let the body's movement drive the blade; fighting the wood with hand strength alone tires quickly and reduces control.
Bevel position and depth of cut

Bevel down (the ground face against the work) produces a shallower, more controlled cut. The bevel acts as a ramp that deflects the blade outward rather than allowing it to dig. This is the correct starting position for most drawknife work and for finish shaving close to final dimension.

Bevel up (flat face against the work) is more aggressive and removes material faster. It is less controllable in depth because the blade tends to follow the grain downward. Use bevel up for roughing work where dimension is not yet close to final, and bevel down for the final shaping passes.

The fundamental skill of drawknife work is reading the grain and cutting with it. On a straight-grained riven billet, the grain runs along the length and shavings will peel cleanly in long strips. On a billet with a curve or a node, the grain deviates and a cut that was running smooth will suddenly dive into the wood or skip up. The signs are: a smooth shaving means the cut is with the grain; a tearing, rough surface means the cut is against it.

When the grain reverses: reposition the work on the shaving horse and cut from the other end, or rotate the work 180 degrees in the clamp. A knot or node in the billet requires very light cuts from multiple directions rather than one heavy pass through it.

To produce a round-section leg or handle from a riven billet: work the four corners off first (the billet is roughly square in section after riving), taking long passes along each corner flat. Once the section is octagonal, rotate and take the eight corners off to produce a sixteen-sided section. At sixteen sides, the section is close enough to round that final smoothing can be done with the spokeshave or sandpaper.

To taper a billet (for an axe handle or a chair-leg tenon end): begin each shaving stroke at the point where the taper starts and pull through to the narrow end. Each stroke removes a thin wedge of material. Check taper progress with calipers or by comparing to a template; it is easy to take too much from the thin end without noticing.

The spokeshave follows the drawknife as a finishing tool. It removes less material per pass but leaves a smoother surface and is easier to control for final dimension. The stock remains clamped on the shaving horse during spokeshave work. Push the spokeshave away from the body with both thumbs behind the blade; the cutting direction is opposite to the drawknife.

Sharpen the flat face of the drawknife on a waterstone (1000 grit) before each session: lay the flat face flat on the stone and work in circular motions until any curl on the edge is removed. Then hone the bevel on the stone and strop both faces on leather. A sharp drawknife shaves green ash cleanly and requires only light body pressure. A dull one tears the wood and requires excessive force; if the wood is tearing, stop and sharpen rather than pushing harder.

attrs.heading: Common problems
attrs.intro: Problems when learning the drawknife and shaving horse.

items.symptom: The clamp releases during a cut
items.cause: Foot pressure on the treadle was reduced or the clamp height is set too high for the work diameter.
items.fix: Stop immediately. Reset the work and re-test the clamp grip before continuing. Adjust clamp height so the work fits firmly with moderate foot pressure on the treadle.

items.symptom: The blade dives into the wood rather than shaving the surface
items.cause: The bevel is up (aggressive cut) and the grain is dipping into the wood in the cut direction, pulling the blade in.
items.fix: Flip to bevel down. If diving continues, reverse the work direction on the shaving horse and cut from the other end.

items.symptom: Long shaving strokes produce a wavy surface
items.cause: Body movement is not smooth; the arms are doing the work rather than the whole upper body driving the pull.
items.fix: Shorten each stroke until the surface is consistent, then lengthen gradually. The pull should come from leaning back from the waist, not from pulling the arms inward.
